summary: American playwright and screenwriter known for terse, profane dialogue. Best known for Glengarry Glen Ross and American Buffalo. Also wrote Oleanna, Speed-the-Plow, and screenplays including The Untouchables and Wag the Dog. Developed 'practical aesthetics' approach to acting with William H. Macy.

works.name: Glengarry Glen Ross
works.description: Mamet's most famous play, winner of the 1984 Pulitzer Prize. About down-and-out real estate salesmen in Chicago competing for 'leads.' The first act is set at the China Bowl Chinese restaurant where Ricky Roma delivers a rambling monologue to the timid James Lingk ('all train compartments smell vaguely of shit'). Aaronow and Moss mock people named Patel as deadbeats; these lines were edited for a 2004 revival but kept in the film. Shelley 'The Machine' Levene begs John Williamson for good leads (can't afford the $100 bribe); Dave Moss coerces Aaronow to steal leads between acts. Williamson inadvertently ruins Roma's sale to Lingk. Roma uses the 'Kenilworth trick' (pretends to need to catch a plane to Ray's wife's birthday). At the end, detective Baylen questions Levene after Williamson catches him in a lie (Williamson reveals the Nyborgs are mentally ill with no money). Roma demands his Cadillac and half of Levene's commissions. Unseen bosses Mitch and Murray run the contest. In the film adaptation, Alec Baldwin's added character Blake yells 'Coffee's for closers!' and shows off brass balls. Also inspired the play Bobby Gould in Hell (double-billed with Shel Silverstein).

clues.clue: Real estate salesmen in Chicago competing for leads; Shelley 'The Machine' Levene, Ricky Roma, Williamson; won 1984 Pulitzer Prize
examples: Down-and-out real estate agents vie for the 'good leads'
Ricky Roma successfully plays the role of the salesman with Lingk, and Shelley Levine tries to buy a set of good real estate leads

clues.clue: Alec Baldwin's Blake added for film: 'Coffee's for closers!'; shows off brass balls
examples: Alec Baldwin, who yells 'Coffee's for closers!' to Shelly Levene
this screenwriter created Blake, who shows off brass balls

clues.clue: First act set in Chinese restaurant (China Bowl); Aaronow and Moss mock the Patels as deadbeats; controversial lines edited for 2004 revival
examples: In a play by this man that opens at a Chinese restaurant, the Patels are mocked
The first act of Glengarry Glen Ross is set in one of these locations

clues.clue: Shelley Levene admits to stealing leads; detective Baylen questions him; Williamson catches Levene in a lie (reveals Nyborgs are mentally ill and broke)
examples: the detective Baylen takes Shelley Levene into a room for questioning after he admits to stealing the leads
Williamson solves it when Levene caught him in a lie

clues.clue: Ricky Roma uses 'Kenilworth trick' to avoid Lingk (pretends to need to leave for party); Williamson inadvertently ruins Roma's sale; Roma demands Cadillac and half of Levene's commissions
examples: Characters in this play use the 'Kenilworth' trick to avoid talking to James Lingk

clues.clue: Roma's monologue opens with 'all train compartments smell vaguely of shit'; uses examples of lottery busboy and married man hit by car to show life is a carnival
examples: A character in this play delivers a speech that begins: '...all train compartments smell vaguely of shit'

clues.clue: Leads compared to 'toilet paper'; Levene nicknamed 'The Machine'
examples: a character indignantly compares some of these things to 'toilet paper'

works.name: American Buffalo
works.description: A play set at Don's Resale Shop. Don thinks he sold a rare American buffalo nickel for too little and plans with Teach and Bobby to rob the customer who bought it. Teach harbors a grudge against the lesbian couple Grace and Ruthie — Ruthie said 'Help yourself' after Teach stole a piece of toast from Grace's plate, and he accuses them of cheating at cards. Bobby was supposed to help but lies about the man leaving with a suitcase; Fletcher (the planned lookout) never arrives. Teach hits Bobby over the head with a 'dead pig sticker' after discovering Bobby was lying. Characters argue about whether the mugging victim Fletcher was taken to Columbus Hospital or Masonic Hospital. 'Action talks and bullshit walks.'

clues.clue: Don, Teach, and Bobby plan to steal a rare American buffalo nickel (title coin) from Don's Resale Shop
examples: He wrote a play in which Don and Teach plan to rob a customer who had gotten a good deal on a rare coin
Bob, Don, and Teach attempting to steal the title coin

clues.clue: Teach angry about Grace and Ruthie: Ruthie said 'Help yourself' after he stole toast from Grace; accuses them of cheating at cards
examples: harbors a grudge against a woman who protested when he stole a piece of toast
another of his characters complains about Ruthie remarking 'Help yourself' after he took a piece of toast

clues.clue: Teach hits Bobby with a 'dead pig sticker' after Fletcher fails to arrive and Bobby is revealed to be lying
examples: Teach hits Bobby over the head with the 'dead pig sticker' after Fletcher fails to arrive at Don's Resale Shop

clues.clue: Argument about Columbus Hospital vs Masonic Hospital for mugging victim Fletcher
examples: features an argument about whether an unseen character who was mugged was admitted to Columbia or Masonic Hospital

works.name: Oleanna
works.description: A two-person play inspired by the Clarence Thomas Supreme Court confirmation hearings. Title from a Norwegian folk song about a composer's failed utopian community. Carol accuses her professor John of sexual harassment and attempted rape after he offers to raise her grade to an 'A' if she keeps meeting with him. At the beginning John discusses 'term of art' and 'easement' (he's buying a house) with his wife on the phone. John has written a book about 'the virtual warehousing of the young.' John eventually violently beats Carol with a chair as his life unravels. Only two characters: the play ends with 'Yes. That's right.' The language of the professor and student switch in relative complexity over the course of three scenes.

clues.clue: Carol accuses professor John of sexual harassment (and attempted rape); John offers to raise her grade to 'A' if she keeps meeting him; John loses tenure; beats Carol with a chair
examples: Carol accuses her egotistical professor John of sexual harassment and assault
John loses his chance at tenure after Carol reports him for sexual harassment

clues.clue: Inspired by Clarence Thomas confirmation hearings; title from Norwegian folk song about a composer's failed utopian community
examples: This play takes its title from a Norwegian folk song and was described by its author as a reaction to Clarence Thomas's Supreme Court confirmation hearings

clues.clue: Language of professor and student switch in relative complexity across three scenes; only two characters
examples: the language used by a professor and that of his student switch in relative complexity over the course of three scenes

works.name: Speed-the-Plow
works.description: A play satirizing the film industry. Title from the Middle English poem asking God to 'speed the plow' (also source of 'Piers Plowman'). Movie producer Bobby Gould green-lights a movie by Doug Brown instead of a movie about the apocalyptic effects of radiation, despite the efforts of the activist Karen.

clues.clue: Satirizes film industry; Doug Brown's movie green-lit over radiation apocalypse film; Karen is activist; Bobby Gould
examples: a play in which a movie by Doug Brown is green-lit instead of a movie about the apocalyptic effects of radiation
This playwright attacked the film industry in his play Speed-the-Plow

clues.clue: Title from Middle English poem asking God to 'speed the plow' (source of Piers Plowman)
examples: David Mamet's play about the movie producer Bobby Gould takes its title from a Middle English poem that asks God to 'speed' one of these objects

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Mamet is known for terse, profane dialogue with characters talking over each other. He developed the 'practical aesthetics' approach to acting with William H. Macy at Goddard College. Joe Mantegna starred in his films Homicide and House of Games. He wrote screenplays for Wag the Dog (adapted from Barry Levinson film, adapted from American Hero), The Untouchables (Brian de Palma), and The Verdict. He adapted Chekhov's Uncle Vanya; Andre Gregory rehearsed it in abandoned spaces shown in Louis Malle's Vanya on 42nd Street. His play The Duck Variations is set on a park bench. His play The Cryptogram, inspired by the divorce of his parents, features Donny discovering her cheating husband has left. Boston Marriage is about two women in New England. The play November is about a president. His book Bambi vs Godzilla is about the movie business. He was a mentor to playwright Neil LaBute. Sexual Perversity in Chicago is another early play.

clues.clue: Terse, profane dialogue; characters talk over each other; 'Mamet speak'
examples: known for terse, profane dialogue
liberal use of profanity and rapid fire dialogue with characters talking over each other

clues.clue: Developed 'practical aesthetics' approach to acting with William H. Macy at Goddard College
examples: this screenwriter conceived the 'practical aesthetics' approach to acting along with his student at Goddard College, William H. Macy

clues.clue: Wag the Dog screenplay (Barry Levinson film); The Untouchables screenplay (Brian de Palma); The Verdict
examples: This author adapted the satirical novel American Hero into a screenplay for Wag the Dog
this dramatist who wrote the screenplay for Brian de Palma's The Untouchables

clues.clue: Adapted Chekhov's Uncle Vanya; Andre Gregory rehearsed it in abandoned spaces, shown in Louis Malle's film Vanya on 42nd Street
examples: Andre Gregory used abandoned spaces to rehearse David Mamet's version of this play, as shown in a Louis Malle film set on 42nd Street

clues.clue: The Cryptogram: Donny discovers cheating husband left; inspired by parents' divorce
examples: Donny finds out that her cheating husband has left her shortly before a planned camping trip

clues.clue: Mentor to playwright Neil LaBute; wrote Sexual Perversity in Chicago
examples: This American playwright of Sexual Perversity in Chicago wrote Oleanna and American Buffalo
This protege of David Mamet who wrote In a Dark Dark House...

comprehensive_summary:
David Mamet is an American playwright and screenwriter known for terse, profane dialogue ('Mamet speak') with characters talking over each other. His most famous play is Glengarry Glen Ross (1984 Pulitzer Prize), which follows down-and-out real estate salesmen in Chicago competing for leads. The first act is set at the China Bowl Chinese restaurant, where Ricky Roma delivers a rambling monologue to James Lingk ('all train compartments smell vaguely of shit') and Aaronow and Moss mock people named Patel as deadbeats (controversial lines edited for 2004 revival but kept in the film). Shelley 'The Machine' Levene begs manager John Williamson for good leads but can't afford the bribe; Dave Moss coerces Aaronow to steal the leads between acts. Williamson inadvertently ruins Roma's sale to Lingk. Roma uses the 'Kenilworth trick' to avoid Lingk. At the end, detective Baylen questions Levene after Williamson catches him in a lie — Williamson reveals the Nyborgs are mentally ill and broke. Roma demands his Cadillac and half of Levene's commissions. The film adaptation added Alec Baldwin's character Blake who yells 'Coffee's for closers!' and shows off brass balls.

American Buffalo is set at Don's Resale Shop, where Don thinks he sold a rare buffalo nickel for too little and plans with Teach and Bobby to rob the customer. Teach harbors a grudge because Ruthie said 'Help yourself' after he stole Grace's toast, and accuses the couple of cheating at cards. Bobby was supposed to help but lies; Fletcher (the lookout) never arrives. Teach hits Bobby over the head with a 'dead pig sticker.' Characters argue whether Fletcher was taken to Columbus Hospital or Masonic Hospital.

Oleanna is a two-person play inspired by the Clarence Thomas confirmation hearings, titled after a Norwegian folk song about a failed utopian community. Carol accuses professor John of sexual harassment and attempted rape after he offers to raise her grade to an 'A' if she keeps meeting with him. The language of professor and student switch in complexity over three scenes. John violently beats Carol with a chair as his life unravels.

Speed-the-Plow, titled from the Middle English poem asking God to 'speed the plow,' satirizes the film industry: movie producer Bobby Gould green-lights a Doug Brown movie over a radiation apocalypse film despite activist Karen's efforts.

Mamet developed 'practical aesthetics' with William H. Macy at Goddard College. Screenwriting credits include Wag the Dog (Barry Levinson), The Untouchables (Brian de Palma), and The Verdict. He adapted Chekhov's Uncle Vanya; Andre Gregory rehearsed it in abandoned spaces, shown in Louis Malle's Vanya on 42nd Street. He is a mentor to playwright Neil LaBute. Other plays include Sexual Perversity in Chicago, The Duck Variations (set on a park bench), The Cryptogram, Boston Marriage, and November.
